title: Returning to a home that no longer exists
summary: A short memoir about going back to a childhood neighbourhood and finding everything — and nothing — the same.
body:
I had not been back for nineteen years. The plane landed at noon, the taxi was old, and the driver, sensing the kind of passenger I was, said almost nothing on the way. He turned, eventually, into a street whose name I had repeated to myself for two decades, and stopped in front of a row of houses I did not recognise.

My childhood home had been replaced by a small office building, painted the indifferent grey that all small office buildings now seem to share. A young woman was smoking outside, looking at her phone. She did not look up. To her, this was simply where she worked. To me, this was where I had learned to ride a bicycle, where my grandmother had cooked the same fish three times a week, and where I had cried, at the age of nine, about something I no longer remembered.

I walked further down the street. The bakery was gone. The pharmacy was gone. The school had been kept, but its blue gate had been repainted yellow, which somehow felt worse than knocking it down. Only one thing was unchanged: the old fig tree on the corner. It was bigger, of course, and bent slightly more to the south, but its leaves moved exactly as they had moved in my memory.

I sat down on a low wall opposite the tree and tried to take stock. I had come, I realised, looking for evidence that my childhood had really happened. I had imagined that a place could keep a record of you the way a photograph does. The street, in this fantasy, would prove that the small boy on the blue bicycle had not been a story I had invented later.

It did not work that way. The street did not remember me. Not because it was hostile, but because places do not, in the end, remember people. They are too busy being themselves, becoming the office buildings and yellow gates of someone else's afternoon.

What surprised me was that this realisation did not feel like loss. It felt, instead, like a kind of relief. If the street did not carry my childhood, then I did. The bicycle and the fish and the unexplained crying were inside me, not behind a door I could no longer open. Memory, I understood for the first time, is not a place you visit. It is a thing you carry, lightly enough that it does not weigh you down, heavily enough that it keeps you who you are.

I got back into the taxi. I gave the driver another address. He nodded. We drove on.

annotations.meaningVi: nhìn lại, đánh giá tình hình

grammarNotes.title: Past perfect for backstory
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong tự thuật, 'past perfect' (had + V3) dùng để kể chuyện cũ hơn so với mạch chính: 'I had not been back for nineteen years', 'My childhood home had been replaced'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Past perfect (had + V3) sets a 'before' layer in narration: 'I had not been back for nineteen years', 'My childhood home had been replaced'.

translationVi:
Tôi đã không quay lại trong mười chín năm. Máy bay hạ cánh lúc trưa, taxi cũ kỹ, và người tài xế, cảm nhận được kiểu hành khách mà tôi là, gần như không nói gì suốt chặng đường. Cuối cùng anh rẽ vào con phố mà tôi đã thầm nhắc tên trong hai mươi năm, và dừng trước một dãy nhà mà tôi không nhận ra.

Ngôi nhà tuổi thơ của tôi đã được thay bằng một tòa văn phòng nhỏ, sơn màu xám hờ hững mà tất cả văn phòng nhỏ giờ dường như đều mang. Một cô gái trẻ đang hút thuốc bên ngoài, nhìn điện thoại. Cô không ngẩng lên. Với cô, đây chỉ là nơi cô đi làm. Với tôi, đây là nơi tôi đã học đi xe đạp, nơi bà tôi ba lần mỗi tuần đều nấu cùng một món cá, và nơi tôi đã khóc, năm chín tuổi, vì một chuyện tôi không còn nhớ.

Tôi đi tiếp xuống phố. Tiệm bánh mì biến mất. Hiệu thuốc biến mất. Trường học vẫn còn, nhưng cánh cổng xanh đã được sơn vàng, không hiểu sao điều đó còn khiến tôi buồn hơn cả khi nó bị đập đi. Chỉ một thứ không đổi: cây sung già ở góc phố. Nó to hơn, đương nhiên, và nghiêng về phía nam nhiều hơn một chút, nhưng lá vẫn lay đúng như cách chúng đã lay trong ký ức tôi.

Tôi ngồi xuống một bờ tường thấp đối diện cây và cố nhìn lại tất cả. Tôi nhận ra mình đã đến đây để tìm bằng chứng rằng tuổi thơ của tôi thật sự đã xảy ra. Tôi từng tưởng tượng một nơi có thể giữ lại bạn theo cách một tấm ảnh làm. Con phố này, trong tưởng tượng đó, sẽ chứng minh rằng cậu bé nhỏ trên chiếc xe đạp xanh không phải là một câu chuyện tôi tự dựng nên sau này.

Nhưng không phải vậy. Con phố không nhớ tôi. Không phải vì nó thù địch, mà vì các nơi chốn, suy cho cùng, không nhớ người. Chúng bận làm chính chúng, bận trở thành những tòa văn phòng và những cánh cổng vàng của một buổi chiều thuộc về ai khác.

Điều khiến tôi ngạc nhiên là nhận ra đó không có cảm giác mất mát. Mà giống một sự nhẹ nhõm. Nếu con phố không mang theo tuổi thơ tôi, thì tôi mang theo nó. Chiếc xe đạp, con cá và tiếng khóc không giải thích được đều ở bên trong tôi, chứ không ở sau một cánh cửa tôi không thể mở. Lần đầu tiên tôi hiểu: ký ức không phải là một nơi để ghé thăm. Nó là một thứ ta mang theo, đủ nhẹ để không đè ta xuống, đủ nặng để giữ ta là chính mình.

Tôi trở lại taxi. Tôi đưa cho tài xế một địa chỉ khác. Anh gật đầu. Chúng tôi đi tiếp.

questions.prompt: How long had the writer been away?
questions.explanation: 'I had not been back for nineteen years.'

questions.prompt: What did the writer find on the site of his childhood home?
questions.explanation: 'My childhood home had been replaced by a small office building.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer notice about the young woman outside?
options: She didn't look up — to her this is just a workplace
questions.explanation: 'To her, this was simply where she worked.'
questions.hint: Look at the contrast with 'to me'.

options: The old fig tree on the corner
questions.explanation: 'Only one thing was unchanged: the old fig tree on the corner.'

questions.prompt: Why did the writer come back?
options: Looking for evidence that his childhood really happened
questions.explanation: 'I had come… looking for evidence that my childhood had really happened.'

questions.prompt: What had the writer assumed about places?
options: They keep a record of you like a photograph
questions.explanation: 'A place could keep a record of you the way a photograph does.'

options: Places do not remember people — they are too busy being themselves
questions.explanation: 'Places do not, in the end, remember people. They are too busy being themselves.'

questions.prompt: How does the writer feel after this realisation?
options: Surprised — instead of loss he feels relief
questions.explanation: 'This realisation did not feel like loss. It felt, instead, like a kind of relief.'

questions.prompt: How does the writer redefine memory?
options: Something you carry — lightly enough not to weigh you down, heavily enough to keep you who you are
Childhood places fade, but the inner record of them remains and is yours to carry
Buildings are more important than people
questions.explanation: The writer accepts that meaning travels with him, not with the street.

questions.prompt: Why was the taxi driver quiet?
options: He sensed the kind of passenger the writer was
questions.explanation: 'Sensing the kind of passenger I was, said almost nothing.'
questions.hint: Look at the very first paragraph.

questions.prompt: What did the writer's grandmother used to cook?
options: The same fish three times a week
questions.explanation: 'My grandmother had cooked the same fish three times a week.'

questions.prompt: What did the writer find 'somehow worse than knocking it down'?
options: The school's blue gate being repainted yellow
questions.explanation: 'Its blue gate had been repainted yellow, which somehow felt worse than knocking it down.'

questions.prompt: How does the writer describe the fig tree now?
options: Bigger, bent slightly more to the south, with leaves moving exactly as in his memory
questions.explanation: 'It was bigger… bent slightly more to the south, but its leaves moved exactly as they had moved in my memory.'

questions.prompt: Where does the writer go after the realisation?
options: Back into the taxi with another address
To stay at the office building
questions.explanation: 'I got back into the taxi. I gave the driver another address.'

blanks.explanation: Replaced by a small office building.
A young woman was smoking outside.
The same fish three times a week.
The street did not remember me.
Memory is not a place you visit.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer says places are 'too busy being themselves' to remember us. If a place cannot carry your past, what does that change about how you should remember — and forget — it?
